items.title: How beer and AI are changing the course of Canberra's economic future

children.content: The capital has long been known as a public service town, but with about two in three workers now employed in the private sector and the population expected to reach nearly 700,000 by 2050, is that reputation — and key economic driver — likely to change?

gallery.altText: A man stands and smiles, leaning on a bench in a large workshop space with buckets and gear behind him.

thumbnail.altText: A man stands and smiles, leaning on a bench in a large workshop space with buckets and gear behind him.

items.title: 'Not what the community wanted': Big Splash lease spared despite backlash

children.content: ACT planning authorities have decided they will not follow through with a threat to terminate the lease of Big Splash water park, saying they are now satisfied the operators have made moves to reopen later this year.

image.altText: Three tower waterslides, all in a significant state of disrepair, surrounded by gum trees.

items.title: Want to buy a football team? Why this club's next chapter is unwritten

children.content: Canberra United is once again without an owner and facing an uncertain future, in a tale all too familiar for players and fans of the A-League's only stand-alone women's team.

image.altText: Soccer players wearing green, black and white uniforms smile at each other during a game

items.title: 'Carry the hope': Thousands gather for Anzac Day service in Canberra

children.content: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and New Zealand High Commissioner Andrew Needs are among those to gather in Canberra for the 111th anniversary of the Gallipoli landings.

gallery.altText: The facade of the Australian War Memorial was lit up before dawn on Anzac Day 2026.

thumbnail.altText: The facade of the Australian War Memorial was lit up before dawn on Anzac Day 2026.

items.title: Canberrans with disability fear NDIS changes will cut vital support

children.content: Canberrans living with disability warn planned changes to the NDIS could strip back support, undermining their mental health and sense of certainty.

image.altText: A woman sitting on a couch looking at the camera.

items.title: Payroll error leaves thousands of hospital staff waiting for fortnightly wages

children.content: Thousands of North Canberra Hospital employees were not paid on time this fortnight, with some staff raising concerns on social media that they had received pay slips but no corresponding bank deposits, after a payroll processing issue.

image.altText: North Canberra Hospital exterior

items.title: 'Self-belief, respect': Indigenous boxers building strength with David Pocock

children.content: A Canberra Indigenous-led boxing camp is helping young fighters hone their skills for life inside and outside the ring in its first development camp, continuing a proud tradition of First Nations involvement in the sport.

gallery.altText: A young boy hits a punching bag in a boxing gym.

thumbnail.altText: A young boy hits a punching bag in a boxing gym.

items.title: The $560k bet on a military raid that exposed a growing online grey zone

children.content: A US soldier accused of using classified information to place bets on a covert military operation has thrown a spotlight on the fast-growing world of prediction markets.

items.title: Sabastian Sawe ran the marathon in an 'unbelievable time'. Here's why we can believe in it

children.content: Sabastian Sawe smashes one of athletics' seemingly unbreakable barriers by running a marathon faster than two hours. Some call it unbelievable, but the Kenyan runner has done everything he can to ensure it stands the test of time.

image.altText: Sabastian Sawe stands next to his record time

items.title: Friday could be Tasmania's hottest May day in nearly 140 years

children.content: Tasmania is well into autumn, but firefighters remain on alert as late-season bushfires have threatened communities. This Friday marks the first day of May, and with it could come one of the state's hottest May days in nearly 140 years of record-keeping.

image.altText: The sun looks like a glowing yellow-white ball in an orange sky above trees that are silhouetted brown-black.

items.title: Citizen's arrest halts alleged tiny home thief in Queensland

children.content: A chance sighting of a social media post detailing the theft of two tiny homes leads to two friends making a dramatic citizen's arrest on the Sunshine Coast.

items.title: Mick Malthouse fought off alleged intruders with exercise bar, court told

children.content: Legendary AFL coach Mick Malthouse "swung an exercise pole" and hit an intruder in the head as he defended his family during an alleged attempted home invasion last year, a Melbourne court has heard.

image.altText: Mick Malthouse is pictured at the MCG

items.title: Maccas wins battle to set up in 'world's coolest street'

children.content: The fast food giant has been cleared to set up an outlet in the Melbourne suburb of Northcote after successfully challenging a council decision to block its restaurant plans.

items.title: Two dead in house fire on day family were due to move interstate

children.content: Two bodies found in a house that was extensively damaged by fire in the foothills of the NSW Blue Mountains are believed to be those of two missing children.

items.title: AFL boss says umpiring 'as good as it's been' despite admitting ARC errors

items.title: 'She's like Will Ferrell': Why Kitty Flanagan said yes to Anne Edmonds

items.title: Paralympic champion shares emotional volatility after traumatic brain injury

children.content: Alexa Leary sustained a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a cycling accident at age 19. The two-time Paralympic gold medallist turned author is calling for a day to recognise TBI and the unique challenges it brings.

items.title: Two young girls die as WA records another dire weekend on roads

items.title: 'Security was extraordinarily lax': ABC's Americas editor on how the shooting played out

children.content: ABC's Americas editor John Lyons said there was less security than your average Australian airport at the event.
He describes people screaming around him as the shots were heard.
Cabinet ministers and other important people were being plucked from tables as the Secret Service started running through the room in "extraordinary" scenes.

items.title: Trump turmoil is political kryptonite in Australia

items.title: Premier says algal bloom 'gone' but fishing restrictions need to expand
